docs.title: Attributes

evidence.docSays:
The `type` field MUST be one of the following:
- `null`
- `boolean`
- `object`
- `array`
- `string`
- `integer`
- `number` (same as `integer`)

issues.suggestion: In the 'Type validation' section of block-attributes.md, add `rich-text` to the bulleted list of valid `type` field values. The `isOfType` function in `packages/blocks/src/api/parser/get-block-attributes.ts` explicitly handles `'rich-text'` as the first `case` in its switch statement, treating it as a fully supported type that validates values as `instanceof RichTextData`.

evidence.docSays: - `html` - data is stored in HTML. This is typically used by `RichText`.

issues.suggestion: Replace the claim that `html` is "typically used by `RichText`" with a statement that `RichText` uses the distinct `rich-text` source type. Additionally, add a separate entry for `rich-text` in the source values list, describing that it stores data as a `RichTextData` object (via `RichTextData.fromHTMLElement`) and is the source type used by `RichText`.

evidence.docSays:
The available `source` values are:
- `(no value)` - when no `source` is specified then data is stored in the block's [comment delimiter]...
- `attribute` - data is stored in an HTML element attribute.
- `text` - data is stored in HTML text.
- `html` - data is stored in HTML. This is typically used by `RichText`.
- `query` - data is stored as an array of objects.
- `meta` - data is stored in post meta (deprecated).

issues.suggestion: The documentation says `html` is 'typically used by RichText', but the actual RichText source value is `rich-text` (handled in `matcherFromSource` and `getBlockAttribute`). Update the `html` entry to remove the claim that it is used by RichText, and add a `rich-text` entry describing it as the source used by RichText components.

positives: The documentation correctly describes that the `meta` source is deprecated and recommends `EntityProvider` and related hook APIs as alternatives — this matches the code's treatment of meta as a legacy feature.

The documentation accurately states that attributes with `role: 'local'` are ignored by the Block Serializer and never saved, which is confirmed by the `getCommentAttributes` function in serializer.tsx: `if ( attributeSchema.role === 'local' ) { return accumulator; }`.

The documentation correctly explains that the `default` field value is used when the `type` and `source` do not match anything within the block content, which is confirmed by `getBlockAttribute` in get-block-attributes.ts: `if ( value === undefined ) { value = getDefault( attributeSchema ); }`.

docs.title: Bindings
positives: The documentation correctly describes that `registerBlockBindingsSource` accepts `name`, `label`, `usesContext`, `getValues`, `setValues`, `canUserEditValue`, and `getFieldsList` — all seven properties are present in the destructuring in `packages/blocks/src/api/registration.ts`: `const { name, label, usesContext, getValues, setValues, canUserEditValue, getFieldsList } = source;`

The documentation correctly states that `unregisterBlockBindingsSource` unregisters a source by name, and the code in `packages/blocks/src/api/registration.ts` confirms: `export function unregisterBlockBindingsSource( name: string ): void { const oldSource = getBlockBindingsSource( name ); if ( ! oldSource ) { warning( 'Block bindings source "' + name + '" is not registered.' ); return; } ... }`

The documentation correctly states that the `block_bindings_source_value` filter was introduced in WordPress 6.7, and the source code in `src/wp-includes/class-wp-block-bindings-source.php` confirms: `@since 6.7.0 \`block_bindings_source_value\` filter was added.`

docs.title: Context
positives: The doc correctly identifies that `providesContext` maps a context name to one of the block's own attributes, which matches the code in `class-wp-block.php`: `foreach ( $this->block_type->provides_context as $context_name => $attribute_name )` — a key-value mapping where the key is the context name and the value is the attribute name.

The doc correctly states that `usesContext` should be an array of context name strings, which matches `foreach ( $this->block_type->uses_context as $context_name )` in `class-wp-block.php` iterating over it as a list.

The doc correctly states that block context is not available to `save`, which is consistent with the architecture: context is a runtime/render-time concept handled in `WP_Block::render()` (PHP) and the block editor React context (JS), neither of which is involved during `save` serialization.

docs.title: Deprecation

evidence.docSays: This function is **not** called when the results of all previous deprecations' save functions were invalid.

issues.suggestion: Remove the sentence 'This function is **not** called when the results of all previous deprecations' save functions were invalid.' from the `isEligible` description. The code only skips a deprecation when `block.isValid` is `true` AND `isEligible` returns `false`; when `block.isValid` is `false` (i.e. invalid), `isEligible` is in fact still called — the `if` guard short-circuits and the deprecation is NOT skipped on that path.

In the 'Changing the innerBlocks' example, add `const { registerBlockType, createBlock } = wp.blocks;` on the destructuring line (replacing the existing `const { registerBlockType } = wp.blocks;`) so that `createBlock` is available when the `migrate` function calls it. Without this, the example will throw a ReferenceError at runtime.

positives: The documentation accurately describes the `migrate` function's return type as 'Either the updated block attributes or tuple array `[attributes, innerBlocks]`', which matches the code in `apply-block-deprecated-versions.ts` that checks `if ( ! Array.isArray( migrated ) ) { migrated = [ migrated ] ... }` then destructures `[ migratedAttributes, migratedInnerBlocks ] = migrated`.

The documentation correctly states that deprecations should be listed in reverse chronological order ('The deprecations in the array should be in reverse chronological order'), which aligns with how `apply-block-deprecated-versions.ts` iterates through `deprecatedDefinitions` from index 0 upward, trying the most recently added first.

The documentation correctly states that `attributes`, `supports`, and `save` are not automatically inherited from the current version for deprecated objects, which matches the code in `apply-block-deprecated-versions.ts` where `Object.assign(omit(blockType, DEPRECATED_ENTRY_KEYS), deprecatedDefinitions[i])` explicitly applies only the keys defined on the deprecation.

docs.title: Metadata in block.json
positives: The documentation correctly identifies `viewScriptModule` as introduced in WordPress 6.5.0 and accurately describes using `wp_register_script_module` — this matches the `register_block_script_module_id()` function signature and the `module_fields` array in `src/wp-includes/blocks.php`.

The documentation's `WPDefinedAsset` object shape (with `handle`, `dependencies`, and `version` fields, including `version` defaulting to `false`) accurately matches the asset file parsing logic in `register_block_script_handle()`: `$script_asset['handle'] ?? generate_block_asset_handle(...)` and `$script_asset['version'] ?? $block_version`.

The documentation correctly states that `viewScript` assets are enqueued only on the frontend and that `script` assets are enqueued both in the editor and frontend — this matches the `$script_fields` array in `register_block_type_from_metadata()` which maps `viewScript` to `view_script_handles` and `script` to `script_handles`, consistent with WordPress's enqueue behavior.

docs.title: Patterns
positives: The documentation correctly identifies `title` as a required property, matching the code which calls `_doing_it_wrong()` when `$pattern_properties['title']` is missing or not a string.
The documentation accurately describes `postTypes` as restricting a pattern to specific post types, which matches the PHP registry's `@type string[] $postTypes` parameter documentation.

The documentation correctly states that `register_block_pattern()` and related functions should be called from a handler attached to the `init` hook, consistent with the registry's internal check using `current_action()` to detect patterns registered outside `init`.

docs.title: Registration

evidence.docSays: The function takes two arguments, a block `name` and a block configuration object.

issues.suggestion: Update the description of `registerBlockType` to state that the first argument (`blockNameOrMetadata`) can be either a block name string OR a `BlockConfiguration` metadata object (e.g., imported from `block.json`). When the metadata object form is used, the second `settings` argument is optional (`Partial<BlockConfiguration>`). The current doc implies the signature is always `(name: string, settings: BlockConfiguration)`, omitting the overload where a metadata object is the first argument and settings becomes optional.

evidence.docSays:
The core provided categories are:

-   text
-   media
-   design
-   widgets
-   theme
-   embed

issues.suggestion: Add `reusable` to the enumerated list under the **Category** section in `docs/reference-guides/block-api/block-metadata.md`. The `DEFAULT_CATEGORIES` array in `packages/blocks/src/store/reducer.ts` defines a seventh entry `{ slug: 'reusable', title: __( 'Reusable blocks' ) }` that is absent from the documented list.

positives: The documentation correctly describes `registerBlockCollection` as taking a `namespace` string and a settings object with `title` (required) and `icon` (optional), which matches the function signature `export function registerBlockCollection( namespace: string, { title, icon }: { title: string; icon?: Icon } ): void` in registration.ts.

The `blockHooks` allowed target values (`before`, `after`, `firstChild`, `lastChild`) correctly match the `position_mappings` array in blocks.php: `'before' => 'before', 'after' => 'after', 'firstChild' => 'first_child', 'lastChild' => 'last_child'`.

The documentation correctly states that `ancestor` was added in WordPress 6.0.0, matching `@since 6.0.0` in `class-wp-block-type.php`: `Setting ancestor makes a block available only inside the specified block types at any position of the ancestor's block subtree.`

docs.title: Supports

evidence.docSays: When the block declares support for `color.gradient`, the attributes definition is extended to include two new attributes: `gradient` and `style`

issues.suggestion: Change `color.gradient` to `color.gradients` (plural) in the documentation. The `hasGradientSupport` function checks `colorSupport.gradients` (plural), not `colorSupport.gradient`. However, note that `hasColorSupport` still checks `colorSupport.gradient === true` (singular) as a broader color-support check, so the documentation should clarify that the specific gradient feature key is `gradients` (plural).

evidence.docSays: When the block declares support for `color.gradient`, the attributes definition is extended to include two new attributes: `gradient` and `style`

issues.suggestion: Replace `color.gradient` with `color.gradients` (plural) wherever it is used as the support key name in the documentation. The `hasGradientSupport()` function checks `colorSupport.gradients` (plural), so the correct support declaration is `supports: { color: { gradients: true } }`, not `supports: { color: { gradient: true } }`.

positives: The documentation correctly describes `color.enableContrastChecker` behavior, including its default value of `true` and that setting it to `false` explicitly disables the checker — matching the code: `false !== getBlockSupport( name, [ COLOR_SUPPORT_KEY, 'enableContrastChecker' ] )`.

The `get_block_wrapper_attributes()` PHP function signature is correctly documented as accepting an optional `$extra_attributes` array, matching the code: `function get_block_wrapper_attributes( $extra_attributes = array() )`.

The documentation correctly states that `color.background` and `color.text` both default to `true` when color support is declared, which matches the code logic: `colorSupport.background !== false` and `colorSupport.text !== false`.
diagnostics: Pass 2 failed for issue in block-supports: TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'trim')

docs.title: Transforms
positives: The documentation correctly describes the `isMultiBlock` parameter behavior: 'If true, the `transform` function's first parameter will be an array containing each selected block's attributes, and the second an array of each selected block's inner blocks.' This matches the code in factory.ts: `blocksArray.map( ( currentBlock ) => currentBlock.attributes )` and `blocksArray.map( ( currentBlock ) => currentBlock.innerBlocks )`.
The documentation accurately states that `priority` defaults to `10`, which aligns with how `findTransform` uses `candidate.priority` via the hooks library whose default priority is 10.

The documentation correctly identifies all six transform types (block, enter, files, prefix, raw, shortcode), all of which are handled in the codebase via the `getBlockTransforms` function in factory.ts which reads from the block type's `transforms` object.

docs.title: Variations

evidence.docSays: which are passed to the [experimental BlockVariationPicker](https://github.com/WordPress/gutenberg/blob/HEAD/packages/block-editor/src/components/block-variation-picker/README.md) component.

issues.suggestion: The documentation's use of 'experimental' is actually accurate — the component is publicly exported as `__experimentalBlockVariationPicker` in `packages/block-editor/src/components/index.js`. The candidate issue should be REJECTED: no change to the documentation is needed.

evidence.docSays: This function accepts the name of the block and the object defining the variation.

issues.suggestion: Update the description of `registerBlockVariation()` to say 'the object or array of objects defining the variation(s)' instead of 'the object defining the variation', to reflect that the second argument accepts `BlockVariation | BlockVariation[]`.

positives: The documentation correctly identifies the `get_block_type_variations` PHP filter hook name, which matches the actual filter applied in `WP_Block_Type::get_variations()` in `class-wp-block-type.php`: `return apply_filters( 'get_block_type_variations', $this->variations, $this );`

The documentation accurately describes the `isDefault` selector behavior: 'The Editor respects the first registered variation with `isDefault`' — the code in `getDefaultBlockVariation` uses `[...(variations || [])].reverse().find(({ isDefault }) => !! isDefault)` which picks the *last* registered one (after reversing), meaning newer registrations win, consistent with the doc's advice to unregister others first.

The documentation correctly describes the specificity mechanism for string-array `isActive` matches since WordPress 6.6.0, which matches the code in `getActiveBlockVariation` in `selectors.ts`: `if ( isMatch && definedAttributesLength > maxMatchedAttributes ) { match = variation; maxMatchedAttributes = definedAttributesLength; }`
